title: Tetrad conversation
topQuestion: What is the central question of this conversation?

cruxes.question: What does McLuhan's tetrad and figure-ground framework reveal about how technologies shape their environments?
How is AI reshaping human cognition, knowledge, and individual expertise?
How is AI transforming communication, including its effects on voice, meaning, and human connection?
How is AI redefining human purpose, work, and the social contract around employment?
How is AI changing the nature, structure, and dynamics of teams and collective work?

cruxEdges.label: The tetrad's four effects (enhance/obsolesce/flip/retrieve) are the analytical lens actually applied in q2 to AI's impact on cognition; q2's findings depend on q1's framework being internalized first.

Claims about AI homogenizing voice (c95, c102) or creating distortion cascades (c103) implicitly apply the q1 figure-ground logic that technologies reshape their environments in ways invisible to those inside them.

Figure-ground and tetrad analysis applied to AI's ground — corporatism (c35), education metrics (c36) — reveals that AI amplifies existing social structures rather than simply displacing jobs, reframing q4's purpose debate.
The tetrad's reversal and retrieval effects are directly invoked in q5 claims (c129 retrieves the ronin, c140 retrieves the garage model), grounding team-structure predictions in q1's framework.
AI's effect on cognition (c74 cognitive surrender, c84 prompting skill) directly shapes communication quality: how people think with AI determines how they write and speak through it.
The cognitive obsolescence of the socialized mind (c89) and erosion of domain expertise (c78) are the cognitive mechanisms that translate into q4's crisis of purpose and eroding social contract.

AI's enhancement of collective cognition (c79) and obsolescence of institutional knowledge (c82) directly feed into q5 debates about whether teams should be advisory/judgment bodies or production units.
Communication distortion cascades (c103) and transparency failures (c104) compound at the team level, making q3's individual communication pathologies into the social fabric problems q5 must address.
The question of human purpose and the eroding social contract (c119, c120, c153) sets the normative stakes for q5: what teams are for depends on what work is for in an AI-saturated world.
Team-level dynamics (c128 cross-disciplinary collapse, c134 shared context urgency) raise unresolved cognitive questions from q2 about whether AI augments or fragments collective knowledge-building.

c7.text: The tetrad method is different from scenario planning; it is a way to engage with something as old as Aristotle's four causes in an intuitive way, engaging very different styles of cognition simultaneously and functioning as an implicitly neurodiverse approach to reality that is more somatic than intellectual.

edges.label: Rubin's visual psychology origin of figure/ground gives McLuhan the borrowed vocabulary he then extends to media environments and active technological processes.
McLuhan's origin as a literary critic explains why Understanding Media's subtitle treats technologies as 'extensions,' a literary-analytical move applied to culture.
The claim that media create active environments logically grounds the fuller description of 'ground' as all enabling systems, relationships, and recursive sub-figures.
The abstract definition of ground as enabling systems is made concrete by enumerating the smartphone's sprawling supply-chain, regulatory, and infrastructural dependencies.
The smartphone's long list of ground elements explains why COVID's supply-chain lesson is so stark: disrupting one node collapses the whole interdependent system.
The recursive character of ground — that removing the figure collapses the ground — follows directly from defining ground as everything the figure requires to exist.
Treating technologies as active environmental processes rather than isolated objects is precisely what figure-ground analysis offers as an antidote to linear cause-effect thinking.
The claim that the tetrad captures four simultaneous effects gains historical grounding from Eric McLuhan's search for physics-like laws that produced the framework.
The tetrad's status as a discovered law of human technology is what explains its exclusive applicability to human-made artifacts rather than animal constructions.
Identifying enhancement as the first tetrad effect is grounded in the claim that every technology amplifies some human faculty, making 'enhance' the natural entry point.
If every technology amplifies a human function, something previously dominant is necessarily displaced, giving the obsolescence effect its logical necessity.
The abstract principle of reversal-at-extreme and retrieval is made vivid by the highway example, where traffic-jam reversal and river-retrieval are concrete instances.
The general principle that technologies flip at their limit is instantiated by the smartphone reversing from connectivity-enhancer to inward disconnection when overused.
The smartphone's reversal into social disconnection explains the empirical resurgence of landlines among families shielding children from that disconnection.
Radio's obsolescence by television followed by its retrieval via the internet exemplifies how obsolescence shifts power dynamics without causing death.
The retrieval effect's character as a mysterious resurgence rather than direct reaction is illustrated by vinyl records returning as a non-obvious consequence of smartphone culture.

The automobile tetrad exemplifies all four simultaneous effects — enhancement of privacy, obsolescence of horse and train, reversal into traffic jams, retrieval of the knight — validating the general principle.
The inner harmonic resonance between the four quadrants explains why tetrad answers can be poetic and why knowing three quadrants often allows inferring the fourth.
The TV-radio-piano chain of obsolescence and shared campfire retrieval demonstrates the cross-tetrad resonance principle — one medium's flip feeds into another's ground.
Context-dependence of figure-ground interpretation qualifies the claim that tetrad answers can be creatively universal, since geographic or temporal shifts alter which quadrant a finding belongs in.
Nostalgia's dual membership in both retrieval and obsolescence quadrants complicates the clean distinction between obsolescence and retrieval effects.
McLuhan's suspension of judgment as an analytical posture (c29: explorer over explainer) is operationalized in c37's claim that the tetrad framework removes judgment from brainstorming.
The claim that McLuhan only described the already-present explains why accurately describing the present appears predictive — the 50-year perceptual lag makes present-description look like foresight.
The cognitive superpower framing of figure-ground exercises reinforces why the framework counters the natural human tendency to analyze technologies in isolation.

The description of tetrads as engaging multiple cognitive styles simultaneously and as more somatic than intellectual aligns with the tetrad's design for simultaneous rather than sequential apprehension.
'Understanding is not a point of view' directly motivates McLuhan's figure-ground approach, where single-perspective analysis of a technology is insufficient and the environment must be mapped.

The claim that historically textured retrieval findings are most potent for creative briefs specifies one mechanism behind the general principle that more indirect tetrad patterns yield richer outputs.
The same McLuhan text yielding radically different meanings across COVID, Metaverse, and AI eras empirically demonstrates that context shifts the meaning of any figure.
Plato's worry about writing as a threat to oral memory is an early historical instance of a new technology obsolescing a prior cognitive practice, illustrating the tetrad's obsolescence effect.
McLuhan's theory that altering one sense reconfigures the whole sensorium explains why synesthesia's rise and neurodivergence's normalization track broader technological change.
Ran's experiential account of compensatory sensory amplification upon blindness provides empirical grounding for the theoretical claim that senses exist in equilibrium.
The distinct neural routing of vision versus audition provides neuroscientific specificity for McLuhan's sensorium theory by showing how different senses produce different cognitive modes.
The historical dominance of Western visual culture through alphabet and print explains the privileged sequential-logical mode that the eye's neural pathway represents.
AI retrieving the Socratic oracle and Library scholar archetype is consistent with it simultaneously making rote memorization and source-hunting obsolete — curiosity returns as sourcing disappears.
The false confidence created by AI-enhanced information access is the mechanism underlying cognitive surrender — users skip effortful thinking precisely because access feels like knowing.

Cognitive surrender's metabolic logic is sharpened by extended mind theory: LLMs collapse external storage tracking, removing the neurological scaffolding that made safe cognitive offloading possible.
Extended mind theory's account of LLMs collapsing locatable external storage explains the mechanism by which AI undermines domain expertise rather than simply supplementing it.
Rapid AI-enabled autodidacticism exemplifies the false-confidence problem: deep three-day immersion provides the feel of expertise without the embodied depth the claim warns against.
While AI obsolesces average expertise and sourcing, c78 carves out frontier expertise as something AI pattern-matching cannot replicate, limiting the scope of obsolescence.
AI's enhancement of collective cognition and question-generation counters c72's framing of AI as cognitively diminishing, suggesting the effect depends on whether AI augments or replaces thinking.
Pharmaceutical AI hallucinating reagents and equipment demonstrates concretely why frontier expertise remains essential as a quality-control layer beyond AI's pattern-matching.
The SOP example provides a specific instance of tacit, somatic knowledge that resists algorithmic capture, grounding the broader claim about AI's narrow definition of cognition.
Defining cognition narrowly to exclude somatic and institutional intelligence explains why cognitive surrender is especially dangerous: what is surrendered includes kinds of knowing AI cannot supply.
AI freeing individuals from cognitively burdensome tasks is a concrete example of the human agency argument: people can choose to redirect freed capacity rather than cede it.
The claim that asking good questions is the key skill for AI-enhanced cognition specifies the mechanism behind AI enhancing the scientific method and collective inquiry.
c84 shows that AI's cognitive effects depend on user skill in framing questions, limiting c72's blanket claim that AI diminishes critical thinking regardless of how it is used.
The coder-as-orchestrator experience exemplifies the agency and empowerment framing of c83 — AI frees skilled individuals from implementation minutiae to operate at a higher level.
Psychological disorientation from intense AI use is a phenomenological symptom consistent with cognitive surrender's prediction that outsourcing thinking to AI destabilizes the self.
AI's tendency to tell users what they want to hear mirrors the social pressure that makes humans avoid verbalizing uncertain decisions, but AI removes the social cost of redirection.
The agency claim is operationalized through prompting skill: users who frame questions well exercise agency over AI's cognitive effects rather than passively receiving them.
The socialized-mind majority is the population most susceptible to cognitive surrender because their regurgitative cognitive style is precisely what AI can fully replace.
The call for a self-authoring adaptive mindset aligns with c78's reservation of irreplaceable value for frontier expertise, both pointing toward meta-cognitive capacity over content knowledge.
AI's ambiguous effect on neurodiverse accessibility raises the unresolved question of how a shifting sensorium intersects with AI's varying affordances for different cognitive profiles.
Framing perpetual AI consultation as a novel human experience reframes c92's evolutionary point: collective decision-making dependency is ancient, but the non-human AI oracle is genuinely new.

Valuing applied over theoretical expertise qualifies the optimism of AI-enabled rapid autodidacticism: even AI-accelerated learning must be grounded in hands-on experimentation to count as real knowledge.

AI reshaping in-person sentence construction is a live instantiation of McLuhan's sensorium theory — a communication technology altering the cognitive and linguistic fabric beneath conscious attention.
Hour-to-hour sensory variability in autistic and ADHD individuals is a micro-level example of the sensorium's context-dependence that c66 flags at a population level.
AI enhancing grammar and broadening modality conflicts with c95's claim that AI kills originality — the same tool simultaneously polishes surface and erodes distinctive voice.
Loss of individual voice through AI writing is the individual-level mechanism behind c102's broader worry about cultural homogenization erasing productive difference.

Framing AI as a speed-amplifier like the printing press downplays c103's distortion cascade, where AI-generated speed compounds summarization errors rather than merely accelerating accurate transmission.
LLMs' preference for simpler language supports c101's claim that AI can reach audiences who struggle with specialized or colloquial register by providing clearer, more accessible prose.
AI's capacity to add neurotypical small-talk or strip neurodivergent directness raises the unresolved tension in c102 about who decides which norm AI regresses communication toward.
The distortion cascade from AI-generated documents being repeatedly summarized is amplified by the parallel shift toward machine-readable content that further strips nuance and human-legibility.
Lack of transparency about AI's role in shaping a message is a specific mechanism by which AI erases the authentic human voice c102 warns is being homogenized away.
The shift of presentation control from creator to consumer is enacted by the move to machine-readable content, where the LLM rather than the author determines how content is rendered.
AI generating convincing style-caricatures of living authors raises the ownership question acutely: if an AI reproduces your voice, whose communication and ideas are they?
Using AI to break blank-page paralysis and reach one's own viewpoint faster challenges c95's absolute claim that AI kills originality, suggesting it can scaffold rather than replace authentic voice.
Power shifts in information dissemination explain why the locus of presentation control moving from creator to consumer generates institutional resistance — it is also a power transfer.
NotebookLM's rapid compression of large content into nuance-stripped audio is a concrete product example of the distortion cascade c103 describes at the workflow level.

Framing social communication breakdowns as an active problem requiring intervention reinforces c102's concern that homogenized AI communication erodes the social fabric holding disagreement productive.
AI enhancing individual somatic and creative purpose directly counters c116's claim that AI renders human utility value obsolete by opening non-utilitarian dimensions of human contribution.
AI obsolescing human utility value is the mechanism explaining why white-collar knowledge work — defined largely by tasks AI can do — loses its identity-grounding function.

The observation that knowledge workers often cannot identify their direct human impact supports c120's claim that the education-to-stable-job social contract was already eroding before AI accelerated it.
The evaporation of the education-employment contract explains Gen Z and Alpha's unambiguously negative view of AI as the proximate destroyer of a social promise they were counting on.
Anti-capitalist sentiment and viewing AI as destructive manifests behaviorally in deliberate electricity-wasting, Snapchat peer-querying, and the Luddite turn c122 documents.
Young people's negative view of AI targets only front-end generative tools, not AI-as-infrastructure, qualifying c121's broad claim that they see all AI as world-destroying.
TikTok's equivalent carbon footprint to ChatGPT, despite not being perceived as AI by its users, challenges c124's premise that young people's definition of AI will straightforwardly broaden.
Framing youth AI resistance as developmentally normal institutional rebellion qualifies c121's severity by suggesting this cohort's hostility may moderate as they mature.
AI retrieving craft and mastery raises the painful paradox c157 names: people who loved a craft for its own sake find no comfort in AI 'retrieving' mastery if their practice is displaced.

Historical Linotype displacement shows that craft retrieval is real but boutique, qualifying c117's positive framing by noting displaced workers rarely earn equivalent livelihoods from artisanal versions.
If AI amplifies corporatism that was engineered to suppress small competition, the social contract's evaporation is not accidental but structurally embedded in the ground AI is enhancing.
Children cheating via AI in metric-obsessed schools illustrates at the educational level the same amplification-of-existing-ground dynamic c35 identifies at the economic level.
AI's forcing of questions about what work is for is the reflective counterpart to c119's empirical observation that knowledge workers have lost a sense of their own contribution.
AI making work fun for many people who shed minutiae directly conflicts with c157's claim that those who love their craft experience AI as robbing them of meaning.
AI-as-equalizer reframes the evaporation of the Ivy League social contract not as loss but as liberation, where internet access replaces credential gatekeeping.
The call to redesign education around life and career stage is supported by neuroscience showing that people returning to education later have better-formed identities and higher retention.
Degree misalignment with actual skill and mounting student debt make the case for life-stage-distributed education a practical economic argument, not merely a developmental one.
Output-based KPIs and vanity metrics already treated workers as factory units before AI; AI's obsolescence of human utility value completes a trajectory those metrics had begun.
The claim that entertainment always educates better justifies c180's liberal arts approach to management education, which blurs the lecture-performance boundary to develop adaptive thinkers.
A post-capitalist AI enhancing leisure is an extreme version of c115's positive claim: freed from utilitarian output, individuals would have maximal agency over creative and purposeful activity.
AI-enabled 21-day flash teams directly conflict with c131's requirement that healthy teams need shared history and trust, which rapid turnover systematically destroys.
The shared-history requirement for real teams grounds c162's analogy of teams as bands rather than ad-hoc project assemblies — chemistry accumulates over time and cannot be remixed on demand.

Framing AI as displacing rather than replacing team collaboration raises an unresolved question about c128's cross-disciplinary collapse: is siloed AI use displacement or a structural replacement of collaborative norms?

Collapsing an eight-step workflow to two or three steps illustrates c136's claim that AI transforms the team's role from executing an industrial linear chain to curating and judging compressed machine output.
Recognizing that old processes gave people structural translation paths explains why c137 argues intentional process becomes more, not less, important after AI eliminates the steps.
Decision-maker empowerment without expertise requirement is the condition that makes unqualified AI-assisted critique possible, as non-experts now hold the chair from which critique is issued.
The urgency of shared context and knowledge vaults in rapidly forming teams is precisely because such teams lack the shared history c131 identifies as foundational to real teamhood.
Defining AI-only-interaction groups as cohorts rather than teams reinforces c131's point that mutual awareness and shared identity — not just task coordination — constitute a team.
The ROI argument for human-agent hybrid teams is strengthened by c141's complementary strategy of hiring beyond-AI-training-edge experts, both pointing toward talent augmentation over replacement.
AI retrieving garage-style interdisciplinary collaboration provides the cultural logic behind why human-agent teams with diverse skilled people outperform functionally siloed configurations.
Organizational anxiety and skill stagnation from AI augmentation is the mechanism producing the bifurcation c143 observes: fearful workers cluster together and fall further behind.

Prioritizing learning appetite over deep functional experience qualifies c141's deep-expertise hiring: different roles may require frontier knowledge while others benefit more from adaptive generalism.
Developer-community excitement about minimal-team building is the entrepreneurial expression of c147's observation that solo-oriented workers see AI as an opportunity to shed team constraints.

The appeal of solo AI-augmented independence challenges c150's claim that the biggest human problems require genuine collective work, exposing a tension between individual agency and collective necessity.
Authentic shared mission and trust as team foundations is amplified by c163's experiential claim that genuine human connection — knowing what drives teammates — is what makes those foundations real.

AI as a transparency serum exposing inauthentic organizational purpose would unmask the lip-service values c148 identifies, making genuine mission-alignment more legible and forcing organizational honesty.

The claim that big problems require genuine collaboration is bolstered by c151's contrast between lateral collaborative teams and pharaoh-style hierarchies, where the latter serves one person's vision rather than collective problem-solving.
The degradation threshold around 150 people supports c167's claim that adaptive network-style organizations are better suited to AI-era work, as smaller adaptive structures stay below that threshold.
The band analogy for stable teams connects directly to c164's empirical claim that top talent stays where they work with great people on interesting problems — band chemistry not project rosters.

Treating team as an ongoing experience across multiple simultaneous memberships is consistent with adaptive organizations where relationships constantly reconfigure rather than fixing into static units.
The observation that most people are not allowed to show their best skill at work is a concrete symptom of the inauthenticity c148 identifies as driving people toward individual pursuits.
The claim that unevolved corporate models are zero-sum games reinforces c136's point that organizations virtualize old structures rather than redesigning for AI's different operational physics.

The decision to focus on team intelligence and collective decision-making in the age of AI directly frames the four tetrad topics — cognition, communication, purpose, teams — the workshop will analyze.
The organizers' informed hypotheses about rapidly changing team technology use provide the empirical basis for the investment decision to focus on team intelligence.
The November collab event testing a collective operating system for AI-era teams is the applied follow-through of the workshop's analytical agenda of four team-focused tetrads.

crossLinks.note: The tetrad's four effects (enhance/obsolesce/flip/retrieve) are the analytical lens actually applied in q2 to AI's impact on cognition; q2's findings depend on q1's framework being internalized first.

Claims about AI homogenizing voice (c95, c102) or creating distortion cascades (c103) implicitly apply the q1 figure-ground logic that technologies reshape their environments in ways invisible to those inside them.

Figure-ground and tetrad analysis applied to AI's ground — corporatism (c35), education metrics (c36) — reveals that AI amplifies existing social structures rather than simply displacing jobs, reframing q4's purpose debate.
The tetrad's reversal and retrieval effects are directly invoked in q5 claims (c129 retrieves the ronin, c140 retrieves the garage model), grounding team-structure predictions in q1's framework.
AI's effect on cognition (c74 cognitive surrender, c84 prompting skill) directly shapes communication quality: how people think with AI determines how they write and speak through it.
The cognitive obsolescence of the socialized mind (c89) and erosion of domain expertise (c78) are the cognitive mechanisms that translate into q4's crisis of purpose and eroding social contract.

AI's enhancement of collective cognition (c79) and obsolescence of institutional knowledge (c82) directly feed into q5 debates about whether teams should be advisory/judgment bodies or production units.
Communication distortion cascades (c103) and transparency failures (c104) compound at the team level, making q3's individual communication pathologies into the social fabric problems q5 must address.
The question of human purpose and the eroding social contract (c119, c120, c153) sets the normative stakes for q5: what teams are for depends on what work is for in an AI-saturated world.
Team-level dynamics (c128 cross-disciplinary collapse, c134 shared context urgency) raise unresolved cognitive questions from q2 about whether AI augments or fragments collective knowledge-building.

momentum.rationale: Q5 is where all four other questions converge: the tetrad framework (q1) supplies the analytical vocabulary, cognitive effects (q2) determine individual member capacity, communication transformations (q3) shape team interaction quality, and purpose debates (q4) set the normative stakes. Resolution of what a team fundamentally is under AI conditions — ephemeral flash unit vs. stable band, human-agent hybrid vs. human-only, judgment body vs. production unit — would unlock downstream decisions about hiring, measurement, organizational design, and the November collab event itself, making it the single highest-leverage question for the conversation.

latentAgreements.note: Across disagreements about AI's threat to expertise, all four claims converge on the view that genuine depth — whether frontier research knowledge, tacit somatic skill, applied experimentation, or beyond-training-edge expertise — retains irreplaceable value that AI cannot replicate.

Despite tension between cognitive-surrender pessimism and cognitive-superpower optimism, c74, c84, and c88 together imply shared agreement that AI's cognitive effect is mediated by user engagement quality rather than being deterministically harmful or beneficial.

Across debates about flash teams versus stable teams, participants implicitly agree that trust, shared identity, authentic mission, and genuine human connection are non-negotiable foundations of effective collective work regardless of team duration or structure.

Despite different framings — homogenization, loss of voice, social breakdown, transparency failure — all four claims share an underlying concern that AI-mediated communication erodes the authentic human signal that makes communication meaningfully connective.

Pessimistic and optimistic framings of AI's impact on work share a latent agreement that utilitarian-output definitions of human value were already inadequate before AI, and that the real question is what non-utilitarian forms of contribution can be cultivated.

factCheckTodos.claimText: Marshall McLuhan wrote Understanding Media, subtitled Extensions of Man; in the front he wrote 'understanding is not a point of view,' meaning understanding requires more than a single perspective.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Verify that Understanding Media: The Extensions of Man contains the phrase 'understanding is not a point of view' in its front matter.

factCheckTodos.claimText: The terms figure and ground were developed approximately 100 years ago by Edgar Rubin from visual/Gestalt psychology to describe the structure of visible phenomena; 'figure' stands for the object of attention and 'ground' refers to everything surrounding it that is part of it but not immediately in the foreground.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Confirm that Edgar Rubin introduced the figure-ground distinction in visual/Gestalt psychology and that his work dates to approximately 100 years before this discussion.

factCheckTodos.claimText: McLuhan said in 1965, 'I don't explain, I explore,' and considered himself an explorer more interested in percepts and perception than in concepts, theories, and ideas; his work is characterized by a sense of play.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Verify that Marshall McLuhan made the statement 'I don't explain, I explore' in a documented source from 1965.

factCheckTodos.claimText: The laws of media were discovered by Eric McLuhan in an attempt to find whether there are general laws of technology analogous to laws of physics; the work grew out of an attempt to write a revised 10th anniversary edition of Understanding Media, which the publisher ultimately declined to publish.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Confirm through published accounts or Eric McLuhan's own writings that the laws of media emerged from an attempt to produce a revised 10th anniversary edition of Understanding Media and that the publisher declined it.

factCheckTodos.claimText: Marshall McLuhan first said 'the medium is the message' in 1958 at a radio broadcasters conference about the rise of television; radio subsequently aligned well with the internet and became globally available in a way it was only local before.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Verify the date and venue of McLuhan's first documented use of the phrase 'the medium is the message,' specifically whether it was at a radio broadcasters conference in 1958.

factCheckTodos.claimText: The landline is currently popular again among families with young children and teenagers who do not want smartphones; a company called Tin Can sells landlines for young kids and teenagers whose families do not want them to have smartphones.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Confirm that a company called Tin Can exists and markets landline or non-smartphone telephone products specifically for children and teenagers whose families want to avoid smartphones.

factCheckTodos.claimText: A course called Understanding Media Intensive was run with three groups over approximately five years, reading McLuhan's book aloud word by word across 36 sessions, spanning the Covid era, the Metaverse era, and the AI era, with the same words yielding drastically different meanings in each context.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Verify that a course called Understanding Media Intensive was conducted in three cohorts over approximately five years, using a session-by-session oral reading format across 36 sessions.

factCheckTodos.claimText: Jacques Lucy Ran, a figure in the French Resistance who went blind at a young age due to a schoolyard accident, wrote about how as his visual faculty dimmed all his other senses came alive; McLuhan used this account as evidence that affecting one sense affects all the senses and changes who a person is.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Verify the identity and biographical details of Jacques Lucy Ran, including his role in the French Resistance, his blindness from a schoolyard accident, and McLuhan's documented citation of his writings.

factCheckTodos.claimText: Different senses take different neural routes before hitting cognition: eyesight is processed through the prefrontal cortex, while auditory perception initially bypasses the prefrontal cortex before coming back around to cognition. The eye experiences reality in a logical, sequential, one-thing-at-a-time mode, while the ear hears from all directions at once in a simultaneous mode, representing two very different modes of culture.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Verify through neuroscience literature that auditory processing initially bypasses the prefrontal cortex while visual processing routes through it, as claimed.

factCheckTodos.claimText: A major use of AI in at least one large company is helping employees who work in a second language make their communication sound more native and colloquial.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Find documented or reported evidence that at least one large company has formally identified AI-assisted second-language communication polishing as a major internal use case.

factCheckTodos.claimText: The average TikTok user's time on TikTok has a comparable carbon footprint to the average ChatGPT user's usage, yet TikTok is itself an AI-driven system that users do not perceive as AI.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Check published carbon footprint or energy consumption estimates for average TikTok usage versus average ChatGPT usage to assess whether they are comparable.

factCheckTodos.claimText: Historically, the Linotype machine eliminated roughly nine out of ten typesetting jobs per newspaper edition. After major technological displacement, the remaining practitioners of a craft often continue as a boutique or artisanal art form, but society does not provide adequate mechanisms to transition displaced workers into such forms, and displaced workers often cannot earn equivalent income from a boutique version of a skill AI has commoditized.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Verify historical records on the proportion of typesetting jobs eliminated per newspaper by the introduction of the Linotype machine, specifically the claim of approximately nine out of ten jobs.

factCheckTodos.claimText: There is a work called Sidious Classroom where the figure and ground concepts are discussed in relation to McLuhan's work.
factCheckTodos.whatToCheck: Confirm the existence of a work titled Sidious Classroom and verify that it contains a discussion of figure and ground in relation to McLuhan's media theory.
